title: Worming chickens
subtitle: Treating a laying flock for roundworm and gapeworm.
excerpt: Hens kept on the same ground year after year build up a worm burden. This covers how to identify the signs of infestation, the two main licensed products available in the UK, how to administer them, and the egg withdrawal periods that follow treatment.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Welfare Code of Practice for Laying Hens (2018). AHDB Beef and Lamb; AHDB Pork treatment guidance adapted for poultry. Smallholder Magazine, worm-management features 2023.

glossaryTerms.definition: Syngamus trachea, a round worm that lives in the trachea of poultry. Infected birds stretch their neck and gasp ('gaping') as the worm obstructs the airway.
Ascaridia galli, the most common intestinal worm in domestic poultry. Heavy infections cause weight loss, pale yolks, and reduced laying.
The number of days after wormer treatment during which eggs from treated birds must not be eaten or sold. Defined per product; must be observed.

recipeTools.notes: For direct-dose wormer if treating birds individually. Water-soluble treatments go into the drinker instead.

content.text: Free-range hens pick up worm larvae whenever they forage on ground that has carried poultry.

eggs survive in soil for years; hens on a fixed run that has held chickens for more than two seasons almost certainly carry a burden. Treatment twice a year (spring and autumn) is the standard routine for a non-rotated run; once a year is usually adequate for hens on a large rotated plot.
Weight loss in a hen that is eating well, pale yolk colour, and a drop in laying rate are the classic roundworm signs in a group.

presents differently: birds stretch the neck and open the beak repeatedly, and may shake the head. Both are treated with the same active ingredient (flubendazole or fenbendazole) so a dual treatment covers both species.

Two licenced wormers are available over the counter for backyard poultry in the UK at the time of writing. Flubenvet (flubendazole) is mixed into feed at the rate given on the packet for seven days; it has a zero-day egg withdrawal period when used as directed. Panacur (fenbendazole) is available as a water-soluble powder; follow the label for dose rate and observe the
specific to the product. Do not use wormers licensed for other species (sheep, pigs) on poultry: the dose rate and formulation differ.

For medicated feed (Flubenvet): weigh the flock to the nearest kilogram by catching each bird and standing on a bathroom scale. Mix the wormer into the daily feed at the rate on the label; the dose is set to the total flock weight. Do not give any other feed during the treatment week so every bird gets the full dose.

For water-soluble products: ensure all drinkers are removed the night before treatment so birds are drinking when the medicated water is offered in the morning. Replace the medicated water with plain water after the stated treatment period.

Worming alone does not solve the problem on heavily contaminated ground. Rest the run for at least six weeks after treatment, or move the flock to fresh ground. Worm eggs in soil are unaffected by the treatment; birds re-infect immediately if they return to the same patch. Lime applied to the rested run at 50 g per m² helps reduce the egg burden in the top layer.
